title: 2027 Kia Seltos first drive gives you plenty of SUV for not much $
gist: Kia's redesigned 2027 Seltos subcompact SUV starts at $24,990 and leans on Telluride-style styling and thoughtful practical features to compete on value, positioned against automakers that instead lobby for tariff protection from cheap Chinese competition.

insights.claim: While some automakers lobby the US government for protective tariff walls against cheap Chinese competition, Kia instead responded by building a more affordable, value-oriented small SUV, the redesigned Seltos, starting at $24,990 plus delivery.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Frames Kia's low-price strategy as a competitive response to Chinese EV/auto pressure, contrasting with peers seeking tariff protection.

insights.claim: The first-generation Kia Seltos was originally designed with the Chinese market in mind, and the US almost didn't get the model at all; the redesigned second generation is more substantial and specifically Telluride-inspired.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Shows how a product originally aimed at one market pivoted to fill a US value-SUV niche once redesigned.

insights.claim: Fully equipped, the Seltos SX with the prestige package (ventilated seats, advanced adaptive cruise control) can reach nearly $38,000, versus a base FWD LX starting under $25,000 — a roughly $13,000 spread across the trim ladder.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Highlights how Kia captures a wide range of price-sensitive and feature-seeking buyers within a single nameplate.

insights.claim: Kia is offering three Seltos powertrain variants: a 2.0L four-cylinder (147 hp/132 lb-ft), a 1.6L turbocharged four-cylinder AWD-only (190 hp/195 lb-ft) at up to $32,790, and a 1.6L hybrid that won't reach showrooms until Q4 and is still undergoing EPA certification.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: The hybrid variant, arguably the most relevant for fuel-economy-focused buyers, is being launched significantly later than the gas variants, delaying a full efficiency review.

insights.claim: The base 2.0L engine returns a combined 30 mpg with FWD or 28 mpg with AWD, while the 1.6L turbo AWD variant returns a combined 26 mpg despite generating substantially more power and torque.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Quantifies the fuel-economy trade-off buyers face for the extra performance of the turbo/AWD trim.

insights.claim: The base FWD LX Seltos weighs just 3,109 lbs, while the top AWD SX X-Line trim weighs up to 3,715 lbs — a curb-weight gap of over 600 lbs that the author says helps offset any performance shortfall of the base engine.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Illustrates that lighter base trims may drive better than their modest horsepower numbers suggest, relevant to how reviewers judge budget trims.

insights.claim: The Seltos retains an array of physical buttons and dials alongside a single 12.3-inch touchscreen with Apple CarPlay/Android Auto, and the author calls Kia's infotainment system one of the less frustrating OEM offerings.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Signals a design choice—keeping physical controls—that runs counter to the touchscreen-only trend in newer vehicles, likely appealing to usability-focused buyers.

insights.claim: The redesigned Seltos offers 39 inches of rear leg room and 27.8 cubic feet of cargo space (64.2 cubic feet with seats folded), which Kia calls class-leading, plus removable cargo hooks that the reviewer says more cars should include.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Cargo and space specs are concrete competitive differentiators in the subcompact SUV segment.
insights.quote: Why don't more crossovers, hatchbacks, and SUVs provide ways to secure cargo? Thankfully the Seltos does.

insights.claim: The digital main instrument display in the Seltos can be partially obscured by the steering wheel, a noted ergonomic flaw in the cabin design.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: A specific usability defect that could affect driver visibility and buyer satisfaction.

insights.claim: Advanced driver-assist features like blind-spot monitors and cross-traffic alerts are standard only from the SX trim and above, meaning budget-focused LX buyers must forgo them.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: Trim-gating of safety features is a relevant buyer consideration when comparing the base LX against higher trims.

insights.claim: The Seltos handles curves without significant body roll, but the suspension transmits road imperfections and generates noticeable road noise, especially noticeable from the driver's seat versus the passenger seat.
insights.speaker: Jonathan M. Gitlin
insights.so_what: A concrete ride-quality trade-off buyers should weigh against the vehicle's low price point.
